entries.title: Scanning web applications - overview
entries.body:
# Scanning web applications: overview

**Burp Scanner** is an automated vulnerability scanner available only in **Burp Suite Professional**. It crawls the application on its own (crawl), builds a map of its structure and endpoints, and then runs a set of automated checks (audit), hunting for vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, XSS, SSRF and hundreds of others.

As it works, the scanner populates **Target > Site map**: all the requests, responses and resources it finds end up there. Along the way, Burp discovers APIs (REST, SOAP, GraphQL) encountered in the traffic.

## Three types of scan

From the scanner launcher (the **New scan** button on the **Dashboard**) three modes are available:

* **Crawl and audit** - the full cycle: first crawling and map-building, then an active audit for vulnerabilities. This is the main mode (see the separate entry).
* **Crawl** - crawling only, without vulnerability checks. Useful when you just need to reconnoiter the application's structure and fill the site map without sending attacking requests.
* **Audit selected items** - an audit of specific, pre-selected HTTP requests without crawling. Handy when you have already found a suspicious request and want to check it specifically (see the separate entry).

## Where else scanning is launched

* **Live tasks** - background tasks that scan traffic right while you manually walk through the application via **Burp's browser** (and also from Repeater/Intruder). See the section on live tasks.
* **API-only scans** - a separate mode for scanning an API by its description (OpenAPI, SOAP WSDL, Postman Collection) without crawling the UI. See the corresponding entries.

## Where to start

```
1. Dashboard > New scan.
2. Choose the type: Crawl and audit (full scan).
3. On the Scan details tab, specify the starting URLs.
4. Choose a configuration preset (for example, Lightweight for fast
   feedback).
5. If needed, set logins (Application login) and a resource pool
   (Resource pool).
6. Click OK - the task appears on the Dashboard, where the progress is also visible.
```

From here, each step is covered in the separate entries of this section: setting the scope, scan configurations, application logins, resource pools, custom checks and live tasks.

entries.title: Crawl and audit
entries.body:
# Crawl and audit

The **Crawl and audit** mode is a full-fledged scan "from scratch": Burp Scanner starts from the specified URLs, crawls the application with its built-in Chromium-based browser (imitating user actions - clicks, filling in forms), builds a map, and then actively checks the discovered points for vulnerabilities.

Launching is done from the scanner launcher. You open **Dashboard > New scan** and go through the tabs.

## The Scan type tab

Choose **Crawl and audit**.

The **BAC false positive reduction** option is also available here - reducing false positives in the search for Broken Access Control using AI. It requires: a configuration that allows crawling without authentication (for example, the **Deep** preset), and configured logins. Additionally, the behavior when AI credits run out is configurable:

* **Pause scan and alert me** - pause the scan and notify (so you can buy more credits);
* **Continue scanning without AI enhancements** - continue without the AI enhancements.

## The Scan details tab

The main thing here is what and where to scan.

* **URLs to scan** - the starting URLs, one per line. The scanner will follow links from these addresses deeper into the application. By default the scope is limited to the directory of the starting URL (more on this in the entry about scope).
* Protocol selection:
  * **Scan using HTTP & HTTPS** - check both protocols;
  * **Scan using my specified protocols** - only those explicitly set in the URL (the scheme is mandatory).
* **Detailed scope configuration** - fine-tuning the scope via URL prefixes or advanced rules (see the separate entry).
* **Run isolated scan** - an isolated scan: its results do not go into the shared **Site map** and are not mixed with everything else on the **Dashboard**. Handy for trying out a configuration.

## The Scan configuration tab

Set *how* the scan will run: take a ready-made preset (**Lightweight**, **Fast**, **Balanced**, **Deep**), create your own configuration or load a previously saved one from the library. See the entry about configuring scans.

## The Application login tab (optional)

So that the scanner can reach the content behind authentication:

* **Use login credentials** - simple username/password pairs for a single-step login;
* **Use recorded login sequences** - recorded login sequences for complex mechanisms (SSO, multi-step forms).

## The Resource pool tab (optional)

Set a resource pool - a limit on simultaneous requests and delays, to regulate the load on the target.

After configuring, click launch - the task appears on the **Dashboard**, where you can see the progress, the issues found and the event log.

## Practice

* For bug bounty on someone else's target, start with a light preset and a careful resource pool, so as not to take down the service and not get blocked.
* Before a full scan, be sure to limit the scope - otherwise the scanner may wander beyond the target (to third-party domains via links).

entries.title: Auditing specific HTTP messages (Audit selected items)
entries.body:
# Auditing specific HTTP messages

Sometimes you do not need a full crawl - you already have a specific request you want to check (for example, you noticed a suspicious parameter in **Proxy > HTTP history**). Burp lets you launch a targeted audit of selected messages from practically any tool where traffic is displayed: **Site map**, **HTTP history** and so on.

## How to launch

1. In the list of requests (for example, in **HTTP history** or on the site map), select one or more entries you want to check.
2. Right-click - the context menu has three scan options:
   * **Scan** - open the launcher with full configuration. Sub-items: **Open scan launcher** (configure before starting) or **Add to task** (add to an existing task).
   * **Do passive scan** - a passive check: it analyzes only the request/response itself, without sending new requests. Safe and fast, but it only finds what is visible "as is".
   * **Do active scan** - an active check: it sends new requests (payloads) to the target to detect vulnerabilities. More powerful, but noisier.

## Configuring via the launcher

If you choose **Scan > Open scan launcher**:

1. On the **Scan type** tab, choose **Audit selected items**.
2. Decide where to place the task: **Add to task** (to an existing one) or **Create new task** (a new one).
3. Optionally enable **Consolidate items** - filtering out redundant messages so as not to duplicate work. You can remove:
   * duplicates by URL/parameters;
   * items out of scope;
   * requests without parameters;
   * requests with the specified file extensions.
4. Configure the rest (scan configuration, resource pool).
5. Click launch.

## Important

* In the **Audit selected items** mode there is no crawl - exactly the selected items and their insertion points are scanned. Accordingly, for this mode you cannot set application logins (there is nothing to crawl).
* If you need to check not the entire request but specific places, use a check with defined insertion points - select the desired fragment before launching.

## Practice

A typical bug bounty scenario: you catch an interesting request in Proxy and send it to **Audit selected items** with active checks. This is far cheaper and more precise than running a full crawl over the whole application when the target is already known.

entries.title: Configuring scans
entries.body:
# Scan configurations

A **Scan configuration** is a set of settings that determine *how* a scan is performed: how deeply to crawl the application, how fast and which checks to run, which issues to report, how to react to application errors, and so on. Configurations apply to both web scans and API scans, and they can be saved for reuse between projects.

## Ready-made presets

Burp provides four built-in modes - you choose based on the balance you need between "coverage versus speed":

* **Lightweight** - fast feedback, the scan fits roughly within 15 minutes.
* **Fast** - broader coverage, usually finishes within an hour.
* **Balanced** - a compromise of coverage and speed, takes several hours.
* **Deep** - the most complete coverage; the time depends heavily on the complexity of the target.

Besides a preset you can choose **Custom** - "start with Burp's default settings and tweak them to suit yourself".

## What can actually be configured

The configuration is divided into two large parts - crawl and audit. In particular, you can control:

* the crawl strategy and depth (how far and how thoroughly to crawl, limits by time/links);
* the speed and thoroughness of the audit;
* the set of active/passive checks (which classes of vulnerabilities to look for);
* which issues to include in the report;
* the handling of application errors (when to slow down or skip);
* insertion points - where exactly to insert the payloads.

## Saving and loading

* **Save.** Having tuned the settings, open the save dialog and give it a unique name (**Configuration name**). You can save a full crawl-and-audit configuration or separate variants for crawl only (crawl-only) or audit only (audit-only).
* **Load.** On the configuration tab in the launcher, click **Load** and choose from the library - built-in or your own.
* **Overwrite behavior (from version 2025.3).** Presets and custom crawl-and-audit configurations overwrite *all* settings when loaded; crawl-only and audit-only overwrite only their own part.

## Configuration library

The **Configuration library** stores both built-in and custom configurations, lets you reuse them across different projects and export them between Burp installations.

## Practice

* For the first pass over an unfamiliar target, take **Lightweight** - you will quickly understand whether there is anything to latch onto at all, and then follow up with a deeper scan on the interesting places.
* Make a couple of your own presets for typical tasks (for example, "injections only, no noisy checks") and keep them in the library.

entries.title: Setting scan scope
entries.body:
# Scan scope

The boundaries of a scan are set on the **Scan details** tab of the launcher and determine where the scanner may go and where it may not. The correct scope is critical: too broad, and the scanner will wander beyond the target (to third-party domains via links, which is unacceptable in bug bounty); too narrow, and you will miss part of the application.

## Starting URLs and automatic scope

For the **Crawl and audit** and **Crawl** types, one or more starting URLs are set. Burp Scanner follows links from these addresses deeper.

By default the scope is limited to the directory of the starting URL, and the boundary is determined by the last slash:

```
Starting URL: https://example.org/myapp/welcome.php
  -> everything within https://example.org/myapp/ falls into scope

Starting URL: https://example.org/myapp/myfolder
  -> everything within https://example.org/myapp/ falls into scope
     (myfolder without a trailing slash is treated as a file,
      the boundary is taken up to the last slash)
```

## Detailed scope configuration

To override the default behavior, enable **Detailed scope configuration**. Then the scope is set in one of two ways:

* **URL prefixes** - by address prefixes (simpler);
* **advanced matching rules** - advanced inclusion/exclusion rules (more precise: by protocol, host, port, path, with regular expressions).

Important: the starting URLs must in any case fall within the project's overall **target scope** (configured in **Target > Scope**). The scan scope cannot extend beyond the project scope.

## Protocols

* **Scan using HTTP & HTTPS** - check both protocols regardless of what is specified in the URL.
* **Scan using my specified protocols** - only the explicitly set protocols.

## URL fragments (#) and SPAs

During a browser-powered crawl, Burp accepts URLs with fragments (`#`). This lets the scanner work correctly with client-side routing in React applications and single-page apps (SPAs). The legacy crawl engine does not support fragments.

## Run isolated scan

The **Run isolated scan** option prevents the scan's results from going into the shared **Site map** and **Dashboard** - handy when you are trying out a configuration separately from the main work.

## Practice

* In bug bounty, always reconcile the scan scope with the program's rules: exclude out-of-scope subdomains via **advanced matching rules** in advance.
* If the application is an SPA, make sure a browser-powered crawl is used (it understands `#`-routes), otherwise the scanner will not see most of the pages.

entries.title: Configuring application logins
entries.body:
# Application logins: overview

Most of the interesting things in an application are hidden behind authentication. So that Burp Scanner can reach content available only to logged-in users, you need to give it credentials - then during the crawl it will authenticate on its own.

Logins are set on the **Application login** tab of the scanner launcher. There are two approaches:

* **Username and password pairs** - simple username/password pairs for a straightforward, single-step login (an ordinary form with two fields). Details are in a separate entry.
* **Recorded login sequences** - recorded login sequences for complex mechanisms: SSO, multi-step forms, forms with additional fields. The recording can be done manually or generated using AI. See the separate entry.

## Important rules

* **For crawling only.** Logins cannot be set for a scan of the **Audit selected items** type - it does not crawl the application, there is nowhere to authenticate.
* **Recording takes priority.** If both simple credentials and a recorded sequence are set, during scanning Burp uses precisely the recorded login.
* In effect, a single login mechanism works per scan - this rules out conflicts between authentication methods.

## Practice

* If the application has an ordinary login form, start with simple username/password pairs, that is the fastest.
* Several accounts with different privilege levels are useful for finding access-control violations (in combination with **BAC false positive reduction**).
* A complex login (captcha, SSO, OTP) almost always requires a recorded sequence - simple credentials will not work here.

entries.title: Adding username/password pairs
entries.body:
# Adding username/password pairs

The simplest way to give the scanner access behind authentication is to set username/password pairs. It works for uncomplicated login forms with two fields.

## Steps

1. Open the **Application login** tab in the scanner launcher.
2. Make sure the **Use login credentials (username & password)** option is selected.
3. Click **New** - the credentials dialog opens.
4. Fill in:
   * **Label** - a clear label to distinguish sets (for example, "admin", "regular user");
   * **Username** - the username;
   * **Password** - the password.
5. Click **OK** to save.

## Managing credentials

* **Multiple sets.** You can set more than one pair per scan - Burp will try to log in as each. This is handy for checking access control between roles.
* **Editing.** Select a set and click **Edit** to adjust the label, username or password. **Delete** removes it.

## Testing login functions

In the **Testing login functions** section there are additional options for testing the login/registration functions themselves:

* attempts at automatic self-registration of a new user;
* sending deliberately incorrect credentials to provoke an intentional login failure (checking the behavior on a failed login).

## Limitations

* This method cannot be combined with recorded login sequences within a single scan.
* It is designed only for simple forms. If the login is multi-step, via SSO, with a captcha or OTP, use a recorded sequence (see the neighboring entry).

entries.title: Adding recorded login sequences
entries.body:
# Adding recorded login sequences

When the login is more complex than an ordinary form - SSO, several steps, additional fields - you need a recorded login sequence. It reproduces the real login process step by step. Management is on the **Application login** tab of the launcher.

There are two ways to record it: let the AI do it automatically or record it manually.

## Option 1. Recording with AI (AI recorded login)

The AI tries to log in on its own and records a working sequence - this saves time and reduces the number of manual errors.

1. On the **Application login** tab, choose **Use recorded login sequences**.
2. Click **New** - the **New Recorded Login** dialog opens.
3. Choose **AI recorded login**, click **Continue**.
4. Fill in:
   * **Label** - the name of the login;
   * **URL** - the full address with the scheme (for example, `http://ginandjuice.shop`);
   * **Username** and **Password** - the credentials.
5. Click **Create recorded login** and watch the login attempts (Burp makes up to three attempts).
6. Click **OK** to save.

Logins recorded by the AI are marked in the table with a special AI icon.

**Limitations of AI recording:** scenarios with opening new windows/tabs and elements in the shadow DOM are not supported. If it did not work out, record it manually.

## Option 2. Manual recording (Manual recorded login)

Manual recording is done with the **Login Recorder** extension for Chrome, after which the script is pasted into Burp.

1. Choose **Use recorded login sequences** on the **Application login** tab.
2. Click **New**, choose **Manual recorded login**, then **Continue**.
3. Set the **Label**.
4. Paste the recorded script into the **Paste Script** field.
5. Click **OK**.

## Editing

Select an entry and click **Edit** to change the login events:

* **Insert** - add a new event;
* **Edit** - modify an existing one;
* **Delete** - remove an event.

You can switch to the raw JSON with the **See as JSON** button and edit it directly, and **View as Events** returns the table view. To delete the entire login completely, select it and click **Delete**.

## Security

The credentials remain local and are not sent to AI services - they are used only for the local generation of the login sequence.

## Practice

* First try AI recording - it often handles a typical SSO "out of the box".
* If it did not work out (captcha, a non-standard widget, shadow DOM), switch to manual recording via the Login Recorder.

entries.title: Application login configuration library
entries.body:
# Application login configuration library

So that you do not have to configure logins from scratch for every scan, they can be saved to a library and reused. The library stores both username/password pairs and recorded login sequences.

## Saving to the library

1. In the scanner launcher, click **Save to library**.
2. The **Save configuration to library** dialog opens.
3. Enter a name - **Configuration name**.
4. Click **Save**.

All the credentials and sequences from the current list will be saved for future scans.

## Loading from the library

Click **Select from library** and choose the configuration you need - Burp will add the saved credentials or sequences to the login list of the current scan.

## Managing via settings

The library can also be opened from the **Settings** dialog, in the **Configuration library** section - this is an alternative way to manage saved login configurations (view, edit, delete).

## Practice

If you regularly scan the same application (or work in a team), a login configured and saved once saves a great deal of time and rules out errors from re-entering credentials.

entries.title: Managing resource pools
entries.body:
# Resource pools

A **Resource pool** is a group of tasks that share a common quota of network resources. Pools let you manage the intensity of requests to the target and prioritize between different scans: for example, one pool for an aggressive scan of your own lab, another for a careful scan of someone else's production application.

Pools are used by scans and **live audit** tasks, but are not available for passive crawl - it does not send active requests, there is nothing to regulate.

## Creating and assigning

On the **Resource pool** tab in the scanner launcher:

1. Choose **Create new resource pool**.
2. Set a clear name.
3. Configure the throttling parameters.
4. The pool is created when the scan/task is launched.

Each scan is assigned a default pool until you explicitly reassign it. An already-running scan or live audit can be moved to another pool: click the edit icon on the task and choose an existing pool or create a new one.

## Throttling parameters

The same settings are available as in the task parameters:

* **Maximum concurrent requests** - the maximum number of simultaneous requests. Fewer = gentler on the server, slower scan.
* **Delay between requests** - the delay between requests; it can be fixed or random (random helps to bypass crude rate limiting and look less "robotic").
* **Number of retries on network failure** - how many times to retry on a network error.
* **Pause before retry** - the pause before retrying.

## Practice

* For bug bounty, set up a separate "polite" pool: 1-2 simultaneous requests plus a delay - this reduces the chance of taking down the target or getting hit by a block/rate limiting.
* For your own ARS3NAL labs, on the contrary, you can raise the number of simultaneous requests and remove the delays - the scan will go noticeably faster.
* A similar pool mechanism also exists in **Intruder** - for coordinating the pace of attacking requests.

entries.title: API-only scans
entries.body:
# API-only scan

When what you have on hand is not a web interface but the API description itself, there is nothing to crawl - it is far more efficient to load the API definition directly and scan right along it. The **API-only scan** mode in Burp Suite Professional does exactly this.

## Supported formats

Burp Scanner accepts three types of API definitions:

* **OpenAPI** (Swagger);
* **SOAP WSDL**;
* **Postman Collection**.

## Steps

1. **Dashboard > New scan** - open the launcher.
2. On the **Scan type** tab, choose **API-only scan**.
3. Load the definition in one of these ways:
   * enter the address in the **Upload from URL** field and click **Upload**;
   * drag the definition file into the **Upload from file** field (drag and drop).
   Burp will parse the loaded definition and extract the API details from it.
4. Check the API details - Burp will automatically fill in three tables:
   * **Endpoints** - the HTTP method, content type, host and path. By default all endpoints are selected; you can deselect unneeded ones with checkboxes.
   * **Authentication** - the detected authentication methods (for OpenAPI and Postman). Credentials for an authenticated scan are also added here (see the separate entry).
   * **Parameters** - the parameter names, values, descriptions and their location in the request (body, query string, etc.).
5. On the configuration tab, choose a preset or a configuration from the library and launch the scan.
6. Assign a resource pool - an existing one or a new one.

## Important

The definition must be valid and parseable - otherwise the upload will not go through. If the file is not accepted, check that it is a correct OpenAPI/WSDL/Postman without syntax errors.

## Practice

* If the target has a public `swagger.json` / `openapi.yaml`, it is a gold mine: load it by URL and immediately get a full list of endpoints and parameters for the audit, without a lengthy crawl.
* Deselect destructive or irrelevant endpoints (for example, ones that delete data) so the scanner does not poke them.

entries.title: Authentication for API scans
Custom scan checks
entries.body:
# Custom scan checks

Burp has many built-in checks, but sometimes you need to look for something of your own - specific to a particular target or a class of vulnerability that is not available "out of the box". For this there are **custom scan checks** (your own checks, including ones written in the **BCheck** language). Any check saved to your custom-checks library is automatically enabled and works on a par with the built-in ones - in both web scans and API scans.

## Where to enable/disable in a scan

1. In the **Scan launcher**, go to the **Scan configuration** tab.
2. Choose the configuration type you need from the drop-down list.
3. In the **Audit configuration** section, open **Scan checks**.
4. Go to the **Custom** tab.
5. Toggle all checks with the **Enabled** switch or mark individual ones with checkboxes.

## The custom checks table

Columns:

* **Enabled** - whether the check is enabled;
* **Name** - the name of the check;
* **Type** - the type: **Active**, **Passive** or **Unspecified**;
* **Check runs** - the run frequency: **Per insertion point** (for each insertion point), **Per request** (per request) or **Per host** (per host).

## Working with the table

* search via the search box;
* filtering with the **Active** / **Passive** buttons;
* configuring sorting and columns;
* unsaved checks are marked with `*`; via right-click you can choose **Save to library** to save them to the library.

## Creating and importing

The checks themselves are created or imported into the library separately (see the documentation on custom scan checks / BCheck). Both manual creation and importing ready-made definitions are supported.

## Practice

* **BCheck** is a simple YAML-like format: you can quickly describe your own check (for example, for a specific header, token or sign of a vulnerability in your target) and plug it into the scanner.
* For bug bounty it is convenient to keep a set of your own BChecks for recurring patterns and reuse them across targets via the library.

entries.title: Extension scan checks
entries.body:
# Scanner checks from extensions

Extensions (including ones from the **BApp Store**) can add their own checks to the scanner. As soon as you load an extension with checks, they are automatically built into Burp Scanner's audit process and work together with the native checks - in both web scans and API scans.

## Automatic integration

When an extension is loaded, all the checks it creates are automatically enabled in the scanner launcher and run on a par with the built-in ones.

## Where to enable/disable in a scan

1. Open the **Scan launcher** and go to the **Scan configuration** tab.
2. Choose the configuration type you need from the drop-down list.
3. In the **Audit configuration** section, open **Scan checks**.
4. Go to the **Extension** tab.
5. With the **Enabled** switch, turn all the extension checks on or off at once.

## An important limitation

You cannot enable or disable the checks of a *specific* extension through this tab - the switch acts on all of them at once. To remove the checks of one extension, you need to disable the extension itself in the extensions manager (**Extensions**).

## The extension checks table

Columns:

* **Name** - the name of the check;
* **Extension** - the source extension;
* **Type** - the type: **Active**, **Passive** or **Unspecified**;
* **Check runs** - the run frequency: **Per insertion point**, **Per request** or **Per host**.

The table contents support column configuration and sorting.

## Practice

* Many popular extensions from the BApp Store (for example, ones for finding specific vulnerabilities) add exactly these kinds of checks - install the extension, and the scanner immediately gets "smarter".
* If an extension produces false positives or gets in the way, remember: you cannot disable just its checks specifically - turn off the whole extension in **Extensions**.

entries.title: Live tasks - overview
entries.body:
# Live tasks: overview

**Live tasks** are scanning processes running in the background while you manually walk through the application via **Burp's browser** (and also send requests from **Repeater** and **Intruder**). They automatically react to passing traffic: they either audit requests for vulnerabilities or populate the site map with the resources they find. Unlike ordinary scans, they do not need a separate "launch window" - they simply work while you explore the target.

## Two types of live task

* **Live audit** - checks each observed request for potential vulnerabilities (an active audit on the fly).
* **Live passive crawl** - passively fills **Target > Site map** with resources extracted from the observed requests (new links, endpoints), without sending new requests.

## Traffic filtering

Live tasks select what to process by two criteria:

* **Tools scope** - which tools to take traffic from: **Proxy**, **Repeater**, **Intruder**.
* **URL scope** - which of these items to process based on their address.

There is optional deduplication - collapsing items with identical URLs and parameter names, to reduce the volume of scanning.

## Configuration

All live tasks can be given a scan configuration applied to in-scope items. **Live audit** tasks are additionally assigned a resource pool; passive crawl tasks are not (they do not send active requests).

## Practice

* It is convenient to keep **Live passive crawl from Proxy** enabled all the time: just walk through the application - and the site map fills itself, plus findings passively surface, without a single attacking request.
* Enable **Live audit** in scope deliberately: it sends active payloads to everything you open - this is noisy and may be undesirable on someone else's production target.

entries.title: Creating live tasks
Task execution settings
entries.body:
# Task execution settings

These settings control how tasks (including live ones) start and when they run. Access is via the gear icon at the top of the **Tasks** panel on the **Dashboard**.

## Task auto-start (auto-starting new tasks)

Determines what to do with a freshly created task:

* **Create new tasks paused** - create them paused (start them manually when you are ready);
* **Create new tasks running** - create them running right away.

## Task scheduling

Lets you create rules for automatically pausing and resuming tasks at a set time. A rule can fire once or repeat at a regular interval. The rule type is chosen from two:

* **Pause task execution engine** - pause the task execution engine;
* **Resume task execution engine** - resume the engine.

A typical scenario: automatically stop scanning at night or during peak-load hours on the target, and start it again in the morning.

## Resource pools

The link to resource pools is also available here - groups of tasks sharing a common quota of network resources (the number of simultaneous requests and/or the request rate). See the separate entry about resource pools.

## Manual control

Individual tasks can be paused and resumed right on the **Dashboard**. For live tasks this means whether to intercept traffic right now: while interception is off, the task ignores all traffic until you turn it back on.

## Practice

* In bug bounty on someone else's target, it is useful to set up a schedule: let active scans run only within the agreed window and automatically pause outside it - less risk of complaints from the service owner.
* If you launch many tasks at once, **Create new tasks paused** saves you from a sudden flood of requests: you create everything, check the scope, then launch deliberately.
